title: Intel Unveils Low-Cost Celeron M While AMD Releases Mobile Athlon 64
author: Jay Lyman, TechNewsWorld
abstract: TechNewsWorld article (Jan 6 2004, Jay Lyman) on parallel announcements: Intel's Celeron M value mobile processor (1.3/1.2/0.8 GHz ULV; $134/$107/$161 in 1,000-unit quantities) to compete with AMD at the low end while differentiating from Pentium M; and AMD's Mobile Athlon 64 — the industry's only Windows-compatible 64-bit mobile processor for mainstream notebooks, supported by 60+ manufacturers including HP, eMachines, Fujitsu. Aberdeen chief research officer Peter Kastner delivers the canonical mobile-segment read: 'Both companies are rightly attacking the mobile PC space, which is the fastest-growing portion of the industry.' And: '2003 proved that laptop sales are price elastic in that $800 to $1,000 laptops are flying off the shelves.' Kastner rejects the 'dumbing down' framing of Celeron M: it's 'simply a low-cost, high-performance microprocessor designed for the wireless world' that 'will be quickly incorporated into value-oriented, under-$1,000 machines.' He calls Mobile Athlon 64 'another respin of AMD's long-term effort to increase laptop market share.' Mercury Research's Dean McCarron frames 2004 as likely 'one of the more exciting years in microprocessors because of all the new products and the positioning.'
importance_rationale: Documents the Jan 2004 kickoff of the sub-$1,000 notebook wave that would dominate PC sales for the next decade. Kastner's price-elastic laptop thesis and the Celeron-M-not-a-dumbing-down reframe became the consensus takes on the sub-$1,000 segment.
relevance_rationale: The sub-$1,000 laptop segment Kastner identified in 2004 grew into the dominant PC category — netbooks 2007-2011, Chromebooks 2011-present, sub-$500 Windows laptops — and is directly connected to the 2020-2025 Chromebook/low-cost-laptop surge during remote learning and remote work. Price-elastic demand at specific price points remains the canonical PC-market framing.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's price-elastic-laptop thesis was strongly validated: sub-$1,000 laptop share grew from ~25% in 2003 to ~60% by 2010 and remains dominant. His 'Celeron M quickly in under-$1,000 machines' prediction was exactly right — Celeron M dominated the $500-$900 notebook segment 2004-2008. Mercury's McCarron '2004 exciting year in microprocessors' was also right: Pentium M-to-Core transition, EM64T Xeon, Athlon 64 proliferation, Sempron launch, iPod G4 release, and the final run-up to Core 2 Duo (2006).
